html:
[607]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 卖花者顾媪 - Refers to an old woman selling flowers.
  • 持一旧磁器求售 - Means 'carrying an old ceramic vessel for sale.'
  • 似笔洗而略浅 - Means 'resembling a brush-wash basin but slightly shallow.'
  • 四周内外及底皆有盷色 - Means 'the inside and outside as well as the bottom are all covered with a glaze-like color.'
  • 似哥窑而无冰纹 - Means 'resembling a piece from the Ge kiln but without crackle glaze.'
  • 中平如砚 - Means 'the center is flat like an inkstone.'
  • 独露磁骨 - Means 'only the clay body is visible.'
  • 边线界画甚明 - Means 'the edges are clearly delineated.'
  • 不出入毫发 - Means 'not varying by even a hair's breadth.'
  • 殊非剥落 - Means 'not at all worn away.'
  • 不知何器 - Means 'it is unknown what kind of vessel it is.'
  • 以无用还之 - Means 'returned it as useless.'
  • 后见广异志 - Means 'later saw the *Guangyi Zhi*.'
  • 载嵇胡见石室道士案头朱笔及杯语 - Means 'which records the story of Ji Hu seeing a red brush and cup on a Taoist's desk in a stone chamber.'
  • 乾巽子载，何让之所见天狐有朱盏笔砚语 - Means 'the *Qianxunzi* records, as seen by He Rang, the story of a heavenly fox possessing a red cup, brush, and inkstone.'
  • 又逸史载叶法善有持朱钵画符语 - Means 'the *Yishi* records the story of Ye Fashan holding a red bowl to draw talismans.'
  • 乃悟唐以前无朱砚 - Means 'thus realizing that before the Tang dynasty, there were no red inkstones.'
  • 点勘文籍，则研朱于杯盏 - Means 'for marking texts, red ink was ground in cups.'
  • 大笔濡染，则贮朱于钵 - Means 'for large brushes, red ink was stored in bowls.'
  • 杯盏略小而口哆 - Means 'cups are slightly smaller and have a wide mouth.'
  • 以便掭笔 - Means 'to facilitate the cleaning of the brush.'
  • 钵稍大而口敛 - Means 'bowls are slightly larger and have a narrower mouth.'
  • 以便多注浓沈也 - Means 'to hold more concentrated ink.'
  • 顾媪所持，盖即朱盏 - Means 'the old woman's vessel is indeed a red cup.'
  • 向来赏鉴家未及见耳 - Means 'previously, connoisseurs had not seen such a thing.'
  • 急呼之来，问此盏何往 - Means 'I quickly called her back to ask where this cup went.'
  • 曰：本以三十钱买得，云出自井中 - Means 'She said: I bought it for thirty coins, and it was said to come from a well.'
  • 因公斥为无用，以二十钱卖诸杂物摊上 - Means 'Because it was deemed useless, I sold it for twenty coins at a miscellaneous goods stall.'
  • 今将及一年，不能复问所在矣 - Means 'It has been nearly a year, and I cannot find out where it is now.'
  • 深为惋惜 - Means 'I deeply regret it.'
  • 世多以高价市赝物，而真古器或往往见摈 - Means 'The world often pays high prices for fake items, while genuine ancient artifacts are sometimes rejected.'
  • 余尚非规方竹漆断纹者 - Means 'I am not seeking items with carved bamboo, lacquer, or broken patterns.'
  • 而交臂失之尚如此 - Means 'and yet I have already lost such an opportunity.'
  • 然则蓄宝不彰者，可胜数哉 - Means 'How many more hidden treasures are there?'
  • 余后又得一朱盏，制与此同 - Means 'Later, I found another red cup with the same design.'
  • 为陈望之抚军持去 - Means 'which was taken by Governor Chen Wangzhi.'
  • 乃知此物世尚多有，第人不识耳 - Means 'Thus realizing that such items are still common in the world, just unrecognized by people.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 哥窑 - Refers to the Ge kiln, known for its distinctive crackle glaze.
  • 乾巽子 - Refers to the *Qianxunzi*, a historical text.
  • 广异志 - Refers to the *Guangyi Zhi*, a collection of strange stories.
  • 逸史 - Refers to the *Yishi*, another collection of historical anecdotes.
  • 叶法善 - Refers to Ye Fashan, a famous Taoist of the Tang dynasty.
  • 天狐 - Refers to a heavenly fox, a mythical creature in Chinese folklore.
  • 朱盏笔砚 - Refers to a red cup, brush, and inkstone, which were used in Taoist rituals.

Proper Nouns

  • 顾媪 - The old woman selling flowers.
  • 哥窑 - The Ge kiln.
  • 乾巽子 - The *Qianxunzi*.
  • 广异志 - The *Guangyi Zhi*.
  • 逸史 - The *Yishi*.
  • 叶法善 - Ye Fashan.
  • 天狐 - Heavenly fox.
  • 朱盏笔砚 - Red cup, brush, and inkstone.
  • 陈望之 - Governor Chen Wangzhi.

[608]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 先師介公野園言 - Formal introduction meaning 'Master Jie, the owner of the Wild Garden, said.'
  • 親串中有不畏鬼者 - Refers to a close friend or relative who is not afraid of ghosts.
  • 凶宅 - Refers to a haunted house or a house associated with bad luck.
  • 多見變怪 - Means 'often sees strange and supernatural phenomena.'
  • 奇形诡状 - Describes unusual and bizarre shapes.
  • 每夜环绕几榻间 - Means 'every night surrounding the bed and desk.'
  • 处之恬然 - Means 'dealing with it calmly.'
  • 然亦弗能害也 - Means 'but it cannot harm him either.'
  • 一夕月明 - Refers to one clear moonlit night.
  • 推窗四望 - Means 'pushing open the window to look around.'
  • 咥然曰 - A vivid expression meaning 'laughing and saying.'
  • 怖我不动，来魅我耶 - Means 'Frightening me so I don't move, are you trying to bewitch me?'
  • 尔是何怪，可近前 - Means 'What kind of ghost are you? Come closer.'
  • 尔固不识我，我尔祖姑也 - Means 'You don't recognize me, but I am your great-grandmother.'
  • 殁葬此山 - Means 'died and was buried in this mountain.'
  • 闻尔日日与鬼角 - Means 'I heard you are fighting with ghosts every day.'
  • 尔读书十余年 - Means 'You have been studying for over ten years.'
  • 将徒博一不畏鬼之名耶 - Means 'Are you just trying to gain a reputation for not fearing ghosts?'
  • 抑亦思奋身科目，为祖父光，为门户计耶 - Means 'Or are you thinking of striving for success in your studies to bring glory to your ancestors and benefit your family?'
  • 今夜而斗争，昼而倦卧 - Means 'Fighting at night and sleeping during the day.'
  • 试期日近，举业全荒 - Means 'The exam is approaching, and your studies are completely neglected.'
  • 岂尔父尔母遣尔裹粮入山之本志哉 - Means 'Is this what your parents intended when they sent you into the mountains with provisions?'
  • 我虽居泉壤于母家 - Means 'Even though I am in the netherworld at my mother's home.'
  • 不能无情 - Means 'I cannot be indifferent.'
  • 故正言告尔 - Means 'So I am telling you straightforwardly.'
  • 尔试思之 - Means 'Think about it.'
  • 言讫而隐 - Means 'After speaking, she disappeared.'
  • 私念所言颇有理 - Means 'I privately thought what she said made sense.'
  • 乃束装归 - Means 'So I packed up and returned home.'
  • 归而详问父母 - Means 'Upon returning, I asked my parents in detail.'
  • 乃无是祖姑 - Means 'There was no such great-grandmother.'
  • 大悔顿足曰 - Means 'I deeply regretted and stamped my feet, saying.'
  • 吾乃为黠鬼所卖 - Means 'I was deceived by a clever ghost.'
  • 奋然欲再往 - Means 'I was determined to go back.'
  • 鬼不敢以力争 - Means 'The ghost did not dare to fight openly.'
  • 而幻其形以善言解 - Means 'But it transformed its shape and spoke kindly to persuade me.'
  • 鬼畏尔矣 - Means 'The ghost fears you now.'
  • 尔何必追穷寇 - Means 'Why do you need to pursue a cornered enemy?'
  • 乃止 - Means 'So I stopped.'
  • 此友可谓善解纷矣 - Means 'This friend can truly resolve disputes.'
  • 然鬼所言者正理也 - Means 'However, what the ghost said was true.'
  • 正理不能禁 - Means 'True principles cannot be stopped.'
  • 而权词能禁之 - Means 'But clever words can.'
  • 可以悟销熔刚气之道也 - Means 'This can teach us how to soften stubbornness.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 先师介公野园 - Refers to Master Jie, the owner of the Wild Garden, a figure of respect and wisdom.
  • 凶宅 - A house associated with bad luck or supernatural phenomena, often linked to death or misfortune.
  • 变怪 - Supernatural or strange phenomena, often associated with ghosts or spirits.
  • 祖姑 - Great-grandmother, a respected figure in the family lineage.
  • 泉壤 - The netherworld or underworld, where the dead reside.
  • 科目 - Refers to the imperial examinations, a system for selecting government officials.
  • 举业 - Refers to the studies and preparation for the imperial examinations.
  • 门户 - Refers to the family or household, emphasizing the importance of family reputation and success.

Proper Nouns

  • 先师介公野园 - Master Jie, the owner of the Wild Garden.
  • 凶宅 - A haunted house.
  • 西山某寺后阁 - A specific location in the story.
  • 祖姑 - Great-grandmother.
  • 泉壤 - The netherworld.
  • 科目 - Imperial examinations.
  • 举业 - Studies for the imperial examinations.
  • 门户 - Family or household.

[609]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 前记 - Means 'previously recorded' or 'previously noted,' used to introduce the content that follows.
  • 阁学 - A term meaning 'scholar' or 'official,' but its exact meaning is unclear without more context.
  • 札公 - A respectful address, likely referring to a specific individual, possibly a scholar or official.
  • 总宪 - Refers to a high-ranking official, specifically the Grand Censor, a key role in the Qing dynasty's imperial examination system.
  • 壬子 - A year in the Chinese sexagenary cycle, corresponding to 1762 in the Gregorian calendar.
  • 三月初十日 - Refers to the 10th day of the third lunar month.
  • 蒋少司农戟门 - Refers to a person named Jiang, who held the title '少司农' (Junior Minister of Agriculture) and was known as '戟门' (Gate of Halberds), likely a nickname or title.
  • 桃花 - Refers to peach blossoms, often symbolizing spring and renewal in Chinese culture.
  • 联坐 - Means 'to sit together' or 'to be seated side by side,' often used in the context of social gatherings.
  • 不诬 - Means 'not false' or 'true,' indicating that the information provided is reliable.
  • 轶事 - Refers to an anecdote or lesser-known story, often of a curious or unusual nature.
  • 蟠其榻上几满 - Describes the snake coiling around the bed, nearly filling it.
  • 火酒 - Refers to alcohol, specifically a strong liquor.
  • 巨碗 - Means 'large bowl,' used to hold the alcohol.
  • 举首一嗅 - Means 'to raise its head and sniff,' describing the snake's action.
  • 褫汝魄 - Means 'to take away your soul,' a superstitious belief in causing harm through supernatural means.
  • 自挝不止 - Means 'to beat oneself continuously,' indicating a state of distress or madness.
  • 媪 - Refers to an elderly woman, often used in a respectful manner.
  • 红果园 - Refers to a place named 'Red Fruit Garden,' likely a specific location near the city of Beijing.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 阁学 - A title that could refer to a scholar or official, but without more context, it is difficult to determine the exact role.
  • 总宪舒穆噜公 - Shumulu was a high-ranking official during the Qing dynasty, known for his role in the imperial examination system.
  • 壬子 - 1762 in the Gregorian calendar, a year in the Chinese sexagenary cycle.
  • 蒋少司农戟门 - Jiang, a person with a high-ranking title, likely involved in agricultural administration.
  • 桃花 - Peach blossoms are often associated with spring and renewal in Chinese culture.
  • 西直门外 - Refers to a location outside the western gate of Beijing, a common reference in historical texts.

Proper Nouns

  • 阁学札公 - A scholar or official, likely named '札公.'
  • 总宪舒穆噜公 - Shumulu, a high-ranking official.
  • 蒋少司农戟门 - Jiang, a person with a high-ranking title.
  • 刘媪 - An elderly woman, the wife of the tomb keeper.
  • 红果园 - A place name, likely a specific location near Beijing.

[610]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 育婴堂、养济院 - Institutions for nurturing infants and caring for the poor, reflecting traditional Chinese social welfare systems.
  • 瞽者 - Refers to blind people, often marginalized in ancient Chinese society.
  • 选人陈某 - A candidate in the imperial examination system, indicating the meritocratic nature of the Chinese bureaucracy.
  • 资斧匮竭 - Means 'lack of funds or resources,' describing a situation of financial distress.
  • 无可告贷 - Means 'no one to borrow from,' indicating a state of isolation and lack of support.
  • 将自投于河 - Means 'about to throw oneself into the river,' a metaphor for despair or the intention to commit suicide.
  • 倾囊以助其行 - Means 'emptied his bag to help him on his way,' describing the act of giving all one has to someone in need.
  • 竟得官 - Means 'finally obtained an official position,' indicating the successful outcome of the candidate's efforts.
  • 荐至州牧 - Means 'recommended to become the governor of a state,' describing the candidate's rise in rank.
  • 念念不能忘瞽者 - Means 'could not forget the blind man,' expressing deep gratitude and remembrance.
  • 自费数百金 - Means 'spent several hundred gold coins out of his own pocket,' highlighting the candidate's generosity.
  • 申漂母之报 - Refers to repaying a great kindness, similar to the story of the old woman who fed Han Xin and later received his gratitude.
  • 偏觅瞽者不可得 - Means 'could not find the blind man,' describing the difficulty in locating the person to be repaid.
  • 并其姓名无知者 - Means 'no one knew his name,' emphasizing the anonymity of the blind man.
  • 乃捐金建是院 - Means 'then donated money to build this institution,' describing the candidate's act of charity.
  • 此瞽者与此选人，均可谓古之人矣 - Means 'both the blind man and this candidate can be called ancient people,' praising their virtuous actions.
  • 众瞽者留室一楹 - Means 'the blind people left a room,' describing the space set aside for worship.
  • 旦夕炷香拜陈公 - Means 'burn incense and worship Mr. Chen daily,' describing the ritual performed by the blind people.
  • 陈公之侧，瞽者亦宜设一坐 - Means 'Mr. Chen should also have a seat beside the blind people,' suggesting equality in worship.
  • 如以其官而祀之 - Means 'if he is worshipped for his official position,' considering the official status as a reason for worship.
  • 如以其义而祀之 - Means 'if he is worshipped for his righteousness,' considering the moral actions as a reason for worship.
  • 此事在康熙中 - Means 'this event took place during the Kangxi period,' providing the historical context.
  • 君瑞告余在乾隆乙亥丙子间 - Means 'Mr. Liu Ruirui told me during the Qianlong years of 1785-1786,' providing the time frame of the recounting.
  • 尚能举居是院者为某某 - Means 'could still name those who lived in the institution,' indicating the memory of specific individuals.
  • 今已三十余年 - Means 'it has been more than thirty years,' providing the time elapsed since the recounting.
  • 不知其存与废矣 - Means 'it is unknown whether it still exists or has been abandoned,' expressing uncertainty about the current status of the institution.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 育婴堂、养济院 - Traditional Chinese institutions for social welfare, reflecting Confucian values of benevolence and charity.
  • 瞽者 - Blind people, often marginalized in ancient Chinese society, with institutions reflecting compassionate social care.
  • 选人陈某 - Candidates in the imperial examination system, indicating the meritocratic nature of the Chinese bureaucracy.
  • 资斧匮竭 - Reflects financial struggles that could lead to extreme measures, such as suicide.
  • 倾囊以助其行 - Act of generosity, a key theme in Chinese moral literature, emphasizing the importance of helping others in need.
  • 竟得官 - Successful career path of the candidate, reflecting the ideal of social mobility through merit.
  • 荐至州牧 - Promotion to a high-ranking official position, underscoring the rewards of virtuous service.
  • 念念不能忘瞽者 - Expression of gratitude and remembrance, a common theme in Chinese literature, highlighting the importance of reciprocity and moral duty.
  • 自费数百金 - Generous donation, reflecting the Confucian value of filial piety and the importance of repaying kindness.
  • 申漂母之报 - Allusion to the story of the old woman who fed Han Xin and later received his gratitude, emphasizing the importance of gratitude and reciprocity.
  • 偏觅瞽者不可得 - Difficulty in finding the blind man, reflecting the challenges of repaying a great kindness.
  • 并其姓名无知者 - Anonymity of the blind man, adding a layer of humility and selflessness to the story.
  • 乃捐金建是院 - Act of building an institution for the blind, reflecting the candidate's commitment to social welfare.
  • 此瞽者与此选人，均可谓古之人矣 - Praise for both the blind man and the candidate, reflecting the Confucian ideal of virtuous behavior.
  • 众瞽者留室一楹 - Space set aside for worship, reflecting the importance of ritual and reverence in Chinese culture.
  • 旦夕炷香拜陈公 - Daily ritual of burning incense and worshiping Mr. Chen, reflecting deep respect and gratitude of the blind people.
  • 陈公之侧，瞽者亦宜设一坐 - Suggestion of equality in worship, reflecting the Confucian ideal of mutual respect and harmony.
  • 如以其官而祀之 - Consideration of the official status as a reason for worship, reflecting the importance of social hierarchy in Chinese culture.
  • 如以其义而祀之 - Consideration of moral actions as a reason for worship, reflecting the Confucian emphasis on virtue.
  • 此事在康熙中 - Historical context of the Kangxi period, reflecting a time of relative stability and prosperity in Chinese history.
  • 君瑞告余在乾隆乙亥丙子间 - Recounting during the Qianlong years, providing a temporal framework for the story.
  • 尚能举居是院者为某某 - Memory of specific individuals, reflecting the personal connections and community ties that are important in Chinese culture.
  • 今已三十余年 - Passage of time, reflecting the enduring nature of the story and the institution.
  • 不知其存与废矣 - Uncertainty about the current status of the institution, reflecting the impermanence of human endeavors.

[611]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 明季 - Refers to the late Ming dynasty, a period of political instability and conflict.
  • 兵亂 - Means 'military chaos' or 'warfare,' indicating the turbulent times.
  • 被掠 - Means 'was captured' or 'was taken by force,' describing the act of being kidnapped.
  • 舊客作 - Refers to an old tenant farmer or laborer, a term used to describe a lower-class worker.
  • 獨輪車 - Refers to a single-wheeled cart, a common mode of transport in ancient China.
  • 崎岖戎馬之間 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'in the midst of dangerous military situations.'
  • 濒危者数 - Means 'nearly died several times,' indicating the dangerous circumstances.
  • 終不舍去也 - Means 'never left until the end,' emphasizing loyalty and persistence.
  • 酬以金 - Means 'rewarded with money,' indicating a financial transaction.
  • 先顿首谢 - Means 'first bowed in thanks,' showing a gesture of gratitude.
  • 故主流离，心所不忍 - Means 'seeing the old master wandering, the heart could not bear it,' expressing empathy and compassion.
  • 岂为求赏来耶 - Means 'how could I come for a reward?' indicating a selfless motivation.
  • 泣拜而别 - Means 'bowed and wept before leaving,' showing deep emotion.
  • 自后不复再至矣 - Means 'never came again after that,' indicating a final departure.
  • 性戆直 - Means 'simple and straightforward,' describing a person's character.
  • 侪辈有作奸者 - Means 'among peers there were those who acted wickedly,' indicating moral corruption.
  • 辄癳癳与争 - Means 'always fiercely contended,' showing a strong and confrontational nature.
  • 为众口所排去 - Means 'was driven away by many people,' indicating social ostracism.
  • 患难之际，不负其心仍如此 - Means 'in times of trouble, he remained true to his heart,' emphasizing loyalty and integrity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 曾伯祖镇番公 - Refers to a respected ancestor named Zeng Bazu, who was known as 'Zhenfan Gong' (a title of respect).
  • 临清 - Refers to Linqing, a city in Shandong Province, known for its historical significance.
  • 宋太夫人 - Refers to Song Tai Fu Ren, likely a respected elder or matriarch in the family.
  • 壬子三月初二日 - Refers to the specific date, March 2nd of the year Renzi (a year in the Chinese zodiac cycle).
  • 陶靖节 - Refers to Tao Qian (Tao Yuanming), a famous poet and recluse from the Eastern Jin dynasty.
  • 浮沉宦海如鸥鸟 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'floating and sinking in the sea of officialdom like a seagull,' describing the ups and downs of a career.
  • 生死书丛似蠹鱼 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'life and death in the world of books like a bookworm,' describing a life dedicated to scholarship.
  • 百年之后，诸公书以见挽 - Means 'after a hundred years, let the gentlemen write epitaphs to remember me,' indicating a request for posthumous recognition.
  • 刘石庵参知 - Refers to Liu Shian'an, a high-ranking official during the Qing dynasty.
  • 上句殊不类公 - Means 'the first line is very unlike you,' indicating a criticism of the self-description.
  • 若以挽陆耳山，乃确当耳 - Means 'if it were to eulogize Lu Ershan, it would be appropriate,' suggesting a better match for another person.
  • 耳山讣音至 - Refers to the news of death of Lu Ershan, indicating a significant event.

Proper Nouns

  • 曾伯祖镇番公 - A respected ancestor named Zeng Bazu, known as 'Zhenfan Gong.'
  • 临清 - The city of Linqing in Shandong Province.
  • 宋太夫人 - A respected elder or matriarch in the family.
  • 李守敬 - An old tenant farmer or laborer who helped Zeng Bazu.
  • 壬子三月初二日 - The specific date, March 2nd of the year Renzi.
  • 陶靖节 - The famous poet and recluse Tao Qian (Tao Yuanming).
  • 刘石庵参知 - A high-ranking official named Liu Shian'an.
  • 陆耳山 - A person whose death is mentioned in the text.

[612]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 申苍岭先生言 - Formal way of introducing the narrator, 'Mr. Shen Cangling says.'
  • 读书别业 - Refers to a separate study or residence for reading and studying.
  • 废冢 - A deserted or abandoned grave or tomb.
  • 莫知为谁 - A phrase indicating that the identity of the person buried there is unknown.
  • 吟哦声 - The sound of someone reciting or composing poetry, often in a low, murmuring tone.
  • 潜听数夕 - Listening secretly for several nights.
  • 无所闻 - Nothing was heard.
  • 泉下苦吟 - Refers to someone who is struggling to compose poetry in the afterlife.
  • 幽明虽隔 - Despite the separation between the living and the dead.
  • 气类不殊 - Their essence or nature is not different.
  • 现身一共谈乎 - Would you appear to have a conversation with me?
  • 冉冉出树荫中 - Slowly emerging from the shade of the trees.
  • 掉头竟去 - Turned around and left.
  • 殷勤拜祷 - Repeatedly bowed and prayed with sincerity.
  • 至再至三 - Again and again.
  • 微闻树外人语 - Faintly heard the voice of someone outside the tree.
  • 感君见赏 - Grateful for your appreciation.
  • 不敢以异物自疑 - Dare not doubt you because of my unusual form.
  • 破百年之岑寂 - Break the long-standing loneliness.
  • 衣冠华美 - Wearing splendid attire.
  • 翩翩有富贵之容 - Gracefully appearing wealthy and noble.
  • 与我辈缊袍 - Compared to my humble clothing.
  • 殊非同调 - Not of the same kind or spirit.
  • 士各有志 - Each person has their own aspirations.
  • 未敢相亲 - Dare not approach you.
  • 惟君委曲谅之 - Only you understand and tolerate me.
  • 怅怅而返 - Left feeling disappointed.
  • 自是并吟哦亦不闻矣 - From then on, not even the reciting sound was heard.
  • 此先生玩世之寓言耳 - This is just Mr. Shen's playful allegory.
  • 此语既未亲闻，又旁无闻者 - These words were neither personally heard nor witnessed by anyone else.
  • 岂此士人为鬼揶揄 - Could this scholar be mocked by a ghost?
  • 尚肯自述耶 - Still dare to describe himself?
  • 鉏麂槐下之词 - A reference to a story in *The Records of the Grand Historian* where a man named Chu Ji encountered a ghost under a locust tree.

  • 人对狼口，宜其不蕃也 - The phrase suggests that being opposite a place named 'Lang'er Kou' (狼儿口) is not favorable for prosperity.

  • 沽字土音在果戈之间 - The local pronunciation of 'gu' (沽) lies between the sounds of 'guo' (果) and 'ge' (戈).
  • 自改门后，人字洼渐富腴 - After changing the direction of the gate, the 'Renshi Wa' (人字洼) area gradually became prosperous.

  • 族兄中涵知旌德县时 - When my clan brother Zhonghan was the magistrate of Jingde County, where '族兄' (zú xiōng) means clan brother, and '知县' (zhī xiàn) means magistrate.

  • 非聘徽州唐打猎，不能除此患也 - Unless we hire the hunter from Huizhou named Tang, we cannot eliminate this problem, where '非...不能' (fēi...bù néng) is a structure meaning 'unless...cannot.'
  • 明代有唐某，甫新婚而戕于虎 - During the Ming Dynasty, there was a man named Tang who was newly married and killed by a tiger, where '甫' (fǔ) means just or recently, and '戕于虎' (qiāng yú hǔ) means killed by a tiger.
  • 尔不能杀虎，非我子也 - If you cannot kill a tiger, you are not my son, where '尔' (ěr) is a second-person pronoun, and '非我子也' (fēi wǒ zǐ yě) means not my son.
  • 后世子孙，如不能杀虎，亦皆非我子孙也 - If any of my descendants in future generations cannot kill a tiger, they too are not my descendants, where '后世子孙' (hòu shì zǐ sūn) means descendants in future generations.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 今一邑一鄉之中，能建廟者萬家不一二 - Rhetorical structure indicating that among ten thousand families in a town or village, only a very few (not more than one or two) can build a temple. Used to emphasize the rarity of such actions.
  • 能立祠者千家不一二 - Similar structure, but referring to building a shrine or memorial hall.
  • 能設主者百家不一二 - Refers to those who can set up ancestral tablets (主, *zhǔ*), again indicating a very small number.
  • 如神依主而不依墓 - A conditional statement meaning 'if the spirit resides in the tablet and not in the tomb.'
  • 是百千亿万贫贱之家，其祖妣皆无依之鬼也 - A conclusion drawn from the previous statements, implying that countless poor and lowly families have ancestors who are 'unattached spirits' without a proper place of veneration.
  • 有是理耶？ - A rhetorical question meaning 'Is this reasonable?' or 'Can this be true?'
  • 知鬼神之情狀者，莫若聖人 - A classical formula meaning 'None knows the nature of ghosts and spirits better than the sage.'
  • 明器之禮，自夏后氏以來矣 - Refers to the ritual of *mingqi* (明器), which are ritual objects used in burial ceremonies, and notes that this practice began with the *Xiahou* (夏后氏), the ruling family of the Xia dynasty.
  • 使神在主而不在墓 - A conditional statement: 'If the spirit resides in the tablet and not in the tomb.'
  • 明器當設於廟 - 'Then the ritual objects should be placed in the temple.'
  • 乃皆瘗之於墓中 - 'But instead, they are all buried in the tomb.'
  • 是以器供神，而置于神所不至也 - 'Thus, the objects are used to serve the spirits, yet they are placed in places where the spirits do not come.'
  • 聖人顧若是颠耶？ - A rhetorical question meaning 'Would the sage act in such a contradictory way?'
  • 衛人之癙離之，殷禮也 - Refers to the practice of burying the body in a separate pit (癙, *shu*) and then placing it in the tomb (離之, *li zhi*), which was a practice of the Shang dynasty (*Yin*).
  • 魯人之癙合之，周禮也 - Refers to the practice of burying the body in a single pit (*癙合之*), which was a practice of the Zhou dynasty.
  • 孔子善周 - 'Confucius favored the Zhou.'
  • 墓之分合，了无所异 - 'If the spirit is not in the tomb, then whether the tomb is divided or united makes no difference.'
  • 有何善不善耶？ - 'What is the difference between good and bad in this case?'
  • 一物之微，尚且如是 - 'Even the smallest object, still it is treated in this way.'
  • 顧以先人体魄视如无物，而别植数寸之木，曰此吾父吾母之神也 - 'Yet we treat the physical remains of our ancestors as nothing, while planting a few inches of wood and saying this is the spirit of our father and mother.'
  • 毋乃不知类耶？ - A rhetorical question meaning 'Is this not a failure to understand the proper categories?'
  • 寺钟将动，且与子别 - 'The temple bell is about to sound; I shall now part from you.'
  • 子今见吾，此后可毋为竖儒所惑矣 - 'Now you have seen me; from now on, you need not be deceived by shallow scholars.'
  • 生匆遽起立，东方已白，视之正其墓道前也 - 'Rise up quickly, for the east is already white (dawn); look, you are now standing before the path to the tomb.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 主 - Ancestral tablet (*zhǔ*), a key object in Confucian ancestor worship. It is believed that the spirit of the deceased resides in the tablet.
  • 明器 - Ritual objects used in burials, distinct from everyday objects. They were used in ancestral rites and were not for the living.
  • 夏后氏 - The ruling family of the Xia dynasty (c. 2070–1600 BCE), one of the earliest dynasties in Chinese history.
  • 殷禮 - The rituals of the Shang dynasty (c. 1600–1046 BCE), known for their elaborate burial practices and divination.
  • 周禮 - The rituals of the Zhou dynasty (c. 1046–256 BCE), which Confucius is said to have admired and promoted.
  • 孔子善周 - Confucius is known to have favored the Zhou dynasty's rituals over those of the Shang dynasty, as seen in the *Analects* and other classical texts.
  • 竖儒 - A derogatory term for shallow or unlearned scholars, often used in classical texts to criticize those who lack depth in their understanding of Confucian teachings.
  • 寺钟 - The bell of a temple, a common symbol of the end of a ritual or the beginning of a new phase (e.g., a farewell).
  • 墓道前 - The path leading to the tomb, often the site of ancestor veneration and ritual offerings.

Proper Nouns

  • 孔子 - Confucius, the great Chinese philosopher and teacher.
  • 殷 - The Shang dynasty, also known as Yin.
  • 周 - The Zhou dynasty.
  • 衛人 - The people of Wei, a state during the Spring and Autumn period.
  • 魯人 - The people of Lu, another state during the Spring and Autumn period.
  • 竖儒 - A term used to criticize shallow or unlearned scholars, often in contrast to the 'sages' or 'scholars of the Way.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 黎荇塘 - A name of the storyteller or recorder, likely a scholar or historian.
  • 囊家 - A usurer or moneylender, often associated with gambling and debt in classical Chinese literature.
  • 博負 - Gambling and debt were common issues in ancient China, especially among the lower classes.
  • 鬻宅之券 - A legal document to sell one’s house, used as collateral in debt agreements.
  • 自縊 - Suicide by hanging, a tragic and common form of self-inflicted death in classical literature.
  • 父歸也 - The father’s return from a business trip, highlighting the emotional and financial impact on the family.
  • 博訴官 - The phrase suggests that legal recourse via gambling or gambling-related contracts was not viable or trustworthy.
  • 紙铤 - Refers to counterfeit or symbolic money, possibly indicating a moral or symbolic lesson.
  • 自疑遇鬼 - The idea of encountering a ghost or supernatural force is a common motif in classical Chinese stories, often used to explain unexplained events.
  • 讣音 - The formal announcement of someone’s death, often delivered by a messenger or through a letter.
  • 殁已數月矣 - The son had already died several months ago, indicating the passage of time and the delayed realization of his death.

  • 圣人之待互乡、阙党二童子也 - Reference to a passage in the *Analects* of Confucius about two boys from *Huxiang* and *Que Party* who were accepted despite their lack of refinement.
  • 讲学家崖岸过峻 - 'Scholars who are overly rigid or haughty.'
  • 使人甘于自暴弃 - 'Causes people to be content with self-abandonment or self-destruction.'
  • 视世道人心如膜外耳 - 'Treat the moral state of society and the human heart as something outside the membrane,' a metaphor suggesting detachment.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 眷一娈童 - Describes a relationship where the man cherishes a young, attractive male.
  • 性柔婉，无市井态 - Describes the boy's gentle and refined nature.
  • 无恃宠纵意 - Indicates that the boy does not misuse his position for personal gain.
  • 泣涕数日，目尽肿 - Describes the boy's prolonged weeping and the physical effect it has on him.
  • 怪诘其故 - Indicates that the man is puzzled and asks the boy why he is crying.
  • 慨然曰 - Indicates that the boy speaks in a somber tone.
  • 吾日日荐枕席 - Euphemism for sexual intercourse; means 'I have been engaging in sexual relations with you every day.'
  • 殊不自觉 - Indicates that the boy was unaware of the nature of their relationship.
  • 昨寓中某与某童狎 - Describes an incident where someone had sexual relations with another boy in the lodging.

  • 丑难言状 - Indicates that what he saw was extremely unpleasant and hard to describe.
  • 与横陈之女迥殊 - Compares the scene he witnessed to a woman lying down, indicating that it was very different and more disturbing.
  • 因自思吾一男子 - Indicates that the boy reflects on his own identity as a man.
  • 而受污如是 - Indicates that the boy feels he has been treated in a degrading manner.

  • 终怏怏不释 - Indicates that the boy remains discontented and cannot let go of his feelings.
  • 后竟逃去 - Indicates that the boy eventually fled away.
  • 或曰：已改易姓名，读书游泮矣 - Reports that the boy had changed his name and was studying in school.
  • 梅禹金有青泥莲花记，若此童者，亦近于青泥莲花欤？ - Suggests a comparison between the boy and a character in a story.
  • 又奴子张凯，初为沧州隶，后夜闻罪人暗泣声，心动辞去，鬻身于先姚安公，年四十余无子。 - Describes another incident involving a slave named Zhang Kai.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一日，其妇临蓐 - Classical expression meaning 'One day, his wife was about to give birth.' '临蓐' is a formal term for the act of childbirth.
  • 愀然 - Classical term meaning 'sorrowful' or 'gloomy in expression,' often used to describe a change in demeanor.
  • 其女乎？已而果然 - 'Is it a daughter? Soon after, it turned out to be.' '已而' is a classical conjunction meaning 'soon after' or 'later,' while '果然' means 'as expected' or 'indeed.'
  • 会官遣我拘张九 - 'When the official sent me to arrest Zhang Jiu.' '会' here means 'when' or 'at the time when.'
  • 初五日以逋赋拘，初八日笞十五去矣 - 'On the fifth day he was arrested for unpaid taxes, on the eighth day he was beaten fifteen times and sent away.' This is a formal legal record-style expression.
  • 乞宽其限 - 'I asked for an extension of the deadline.' '乞' means 'to beg' or 'to request.'
  • 检征比册 - 'The official checked the tax records.' '征比册' is a bureaucratic term referring to the official records of taxes and assessments.
  • 杖而遣之 - 'He was beaten and then sent away.' '杖' here means to beat with a stick or to punish with a beating.
  • 其实别一张九，吾借以支吾得免也 - 'In fact, it was a different Zhang Jiu; I used this to cover up and avoid punishment.' '支吾' is an idiom meaning 'to stammer' or 'to evade with vague words.'
  • 去岁闻此妇死 - 'Last year I heard that this woman had died.' '去岁' is a formal term for 'last year.'
  • 昨夜梦其向我拜 - 'Last night I dreamed that she bowed to me.' '向' here means 'toward' or 'facing.'
  • 知其转生为我女也 - 'I knew that she had been reborn as my daughter.' '转生' is a Buddhist term meaning 'reincarnation.'
  • 后此女嫁为贾人妇 - 'Later, this daughter married a merchant.' '贾人' is a term for a merchant or trader.
  • 凯夫妇老且病，竟赖其孝养以终 - 'As Kai and his wife grew old and sick, they ultimately relied on her filial care to live out their days.' '赖' means 'rely on,' and '孝养' is a classical term for filial care and support.
  • 杨椒山有罗刹成佛记 - 'Yang Jiao Shan has a record called *The Demon Becomes a Buddha*.' '罗刹' is a Sanskrit term for a demon or a fierce spirit in Buddhist tradition; '成佛' means 'to become a Buddha.'
  • 若此奴者，亦近于罗刹成佛欤？ - 'Is this servant also close to being a *demon who becomes a Buddha*?' '若' here means 'like' or 'as for'; '近于' means 'close to' or 'similar to.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 临蓐 - A classical term used in medical or literary contexts to denote the act of childbirth. It is more formal than the modern term.
  • 逋赋 - 'Unpaid taxes' or 'tax evasion.' This is a term from the imperial tax system.
  • 笞十五 - 'Beaten fifteen times with the bamboo rod.' This is a classical form of corporal punishment.
  • 征比册 - Refers to the official tax and legal records used by the government. This is a bureaucratic term from the imperial administration.
  • 罗刹成佛 - A reference to the Buddhist concept of transformation from a demon (罗刹) to a Buddha. This is likely a title of a story or moral fable, possibly from the works of Yang Jiao Shan (杨椒山), a historical figure and scholar.
  • 贾人 - A term for a merchant or trader, often used in classical texts to denote someone in commerce, which was considered a lower social class in Confucian terms.
  • 孝养 - A classical term for filial piety and care, especially in the context of elderly parents.
  • 杨椒山 - Likely refers to Yang Jiao Shan (杨椒山), a historical figure and scholar from the Ming or Qing dynasty, known for his moral and literary works.

Proper Nouns

  • 凯 - Likely a surname, possibly a fictional or historical character. Rendered as 'Kai' in English.
  • 张九 - A common name in classical Chinese texts, likely a fictional or historical figure. Rendered as 'Zhang Jiu.'
  • 杨椒山 - A historical figure, likely Yang Jiao Shan (杨椒山), a scholar and writer from the Ming or Qing dynasty. Rendered as 'Yang Jiao Shan.'
  • 罗刹成佛记 - A title of a story or moral fable. Rendered as *The Demon Becomes a Buddha*.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 冯平宇言 - Introduces the narrative as a report or statement by Feng Pingyu, functioning similarly to 'It is said that' or 'According to Feng Pingyu.'
  • 佣作 - Means to work as a laborer or hire oneself out for work, often in a menial or manual capacity.
  • 流转至万全山中 - '流转' means to move about or wander; '万全山' is a place name. Together, it suggests the man wandered to a remote mountain area.
  • 遇翁妪留治圃 - '遇' is to meet; '翁妪' refers to an elderly couple; '留治圃' means to be left in charge of tending the garden or orchard.
  • 赘之 - To take a woman as a wife in a situation where the husband moves into the wife’s family home.
  • 越数岁 - 'After several years' or 'a few years later.'
  • 省长女 - '省' means to visit or go to see; '长女' is the eldest daughter. The couple went to visit their eldest daughter.
  • 挈妇他适 - '挈' means to take with; '妇' is wife; '他适' means to go elsewhere. 'Took his wife to another place.'
  • 渐觉其为狐 - Gradually realized that she was a fox. A common motif in classical Chinese ghost and fox tales.
  • 耻与异类偶 - 'Shame to be paired with a being of a different species.' '偶' here refers to being a couple or partner.
  • 潜弯弧射之 - '悄悄地拉弓射箭射向她.' '潜' means secretly or stealthily; '弯弧' is to bend the bow; '射之' is to shoot at her.
  • 中左股 - 'Hit her left thigh.' A literal translation, but in classical Chinese, body parts are often mentioned in this way.
  • 狐女以手拔矢 - 'The fox-woman pulled out the arrow with her hand.'
  • 一跃直至四喜前 - 'Leapt directly in front of Si Xi.' A dramatic and vivid action.
  • 持矢数之曰 - 'Held the arrow and recounted to him, saying...' '数之' is to recount or enumerate, but in this context, it is used in the sense of accusing or listing charges.
  • 君太负心，殊使人恨 - 'You are too unfaithful, which greatly makes one resentful.' A direct accusation of betrayal.
  • 虽然，他狐媚人，苟且野合耳 - 'Although, other foxes seduce people and engage in casual, unchaste unions.' This is a contrast to the speaker’s own situation.
  • 我则父母所命，以礼结婚，有夫妇之义焉 - 'But I was married to you by the command of my parents, through proper rites, and we had the duties of husband and wife.' This is a classical reference to the 'three bonds.'
  • 三纲所系 - 'Bound by the three bonds.' The three bonds (父为子纲, 君为臣纲, 夫为妻纲) are a central Confucian concept.
  • 不敢仇君 - 'I dare not hold a grudge against you.'
  • 君既见弃，亦不敢强住聒君 - 'Since you have cast me aside, I also dare not stay and bother you.'
  • 握四喜之手，痛哭，逾数刻，乃蹶然逝 - 'She took Si Xi’s hand, wept bitterly, for several moments, and then suddenly vanished.' A dramatic and emotional closing.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 狐 - 'Fox' in Chinese folklore is often associated with shapeshifting, seduction, and supernatural beings. In classical Chinese literature, fox spirits (狐仙) are common characters who can transform into humans and often fall in love with human men.
  • 赘之 - This is a traditional form of marriage in which the groom moves into the bride’s household. It is distinct from the more common practice of the bride moving into the groom’s household.
  • 三纲 - The 'Three Bonds' (父为子纲, 君为臣纲, 夫为妻纲) are a central Confucian concept that defines hierarchical relationships in society.
  • 夫妇之义 - The duties and obligations of husband and wife according to Confucian values.
  • 万全山 - A place name, likely a fictional or local mountain. In classical Chinese stories, such names are often used to create a sense of isolation or mystery.
  • 冯平宇 - A name, likely a storyteller or recorder of folktales. In classical Chinese literature, such names are often used to introduce folktales or supernatural stories.

Proper Nouns

  • 冯平宇 - A name, likely the narrator or storyteller. Should be translated as 'Feng Pingyu' or 'Feng Pingyu said.'
  • 张四喜 - A character name. Should be translated as 'Zhang Sihxi' or 'Zhang Si Xi.'
  • 万全山 - A place name. Should be translated as 'Wanquan Mountain.'
  • 三纲 - A canonical Confucian term. Should be translated as 'the Three Bonds' or 'the three hierarchical relationships.'
  • 夫妇之义 - A canonical term referring to the moral duties of husband and wife. Should be translated as 'the duties of husband and wife' or 'the obligations of a marital relationship.'

  • 忘其何地人 - Indicates that the person's origin is unknown or forgotten.

  • 挥金如土 - A common idiom meaning 'to spend money extravagantly,' likening spending to throwing gold into the ground.

  • 诟谇使去 - Means 'scolded him and demanded he leave,' with '诟谇' referring to harsh criticism or scolding.
  • 微是人无此日 - Means 'without that person, I would not have this day,' with '微' meaning 'without' and '是人' referring to the Western merchant.
  • 负之不祥 - Means 'it would be unlucky to betray him,' with '负' suggesting betrayal or debt.
  • 坚不可 - Means 'firmly refused,' with '坚' indicating firmness and '不可' meaning 'cannot' or 'will not.'

  • 谈滇黔之俗 - Refers to discussing the customs of Yunnan and Guizhou, which are distant regions from the speaker's location.
  • 不如吾所知之确 - Means 'not as accurate as what I know,' implying a comparison of knowledge.
  • 晚出数十年 - Means 'born decades later,' used to indicate a significant time gap.
  • 髫龀之子 - Refers to a young child, specifically one with tufts of hair, indicating youth.
  • 论耆旧之事 - Means 'to discuss the affairs of the elderly or past,' implying a contrast between older and younger generations.
  • 见其人者 - Means 'those who have seen the person,' referring to firsthand witnesses.
  • 左丘明身为鲁史 - Refers to Zuo Qiuming, a historian from the state of Lu, emphasizing his role as an official historian.
  • 亲见圣人 - Means 'personally saw the sage,' referring to Confucius, who lived in the same era as Zuo Qiuming.
  • 确有源委 - Means 'has a clear origin and development,' indicating a well-documented lineage.
  • 唐中叶 - Refers to the mid-Tang Dynasty, a specific historical period.
  • 陆淳辈 - Refers to Lu Chun and others, indicating a group of scholars.
  • 持异论 - Means 'hold different views,' suggesting disagreement.
  • 宋孙复以后哄然佐斗 - Refers to the period after Song Dynasty scholar Sun Fu, when scholars began to engage in heated debates.
  • 诸说争鸣 - Means 'various theories contended,' indicating a period of intellectual diversity.
  • 左氏不可信 - Means 'the Zuo Zhuan is not credible,' a common criticism of the text.
  • 吾说可信 - Means 'my theory is credible,' a claim made by critics.
  • 何以异于是耶 - Means 'how is this any different from that?' a rhetorical question used to draw a parallel.
  • 盖汉儒之学务实 - Means 'the study of the Han Confucians was practical,' contrasting with later scholarship.
  • 宋儒则近名 - Means 'the Song Confucians were more concerned with reputation,' suggesting a shift in scholarly focus.
  • 不出新义 - Means 'do not propose new ideas,' indicating a lack of innovation.
  • 不排旧说 - Means 'do not reject old theories,' suggesting a conservative approach.
  • 诸经训诂 - Refers to the interpretation of classical texts, a common scholarly activity.
  • 皆可以口辩相争 - Means 'can all be debated verbally,' indicating the nature of scholarly discourse.
  • 厘然 - Means 'clearly defined,' indicating a clear distinction.
  • 难于变乱 - Means 'difficult to alter,' suggesting the stability of the text.
  • 谓左氏为楚人 - Means 'claim that Zuo Qiuming was from Chu,' a specific criticism.
  • 为七国初人 - Means 'was from the early Warring States period,' another criticism.
  • 为秦人 - Means 'was from Qin,' yet another criticism.
  • 身为鲁史，亲见圣人之说摇 - Means 'the claim that he was a historian of Lu and personally saw the sage is shaken,' indicating a loss of credibility.
  • 既非身为鲁史，亲见圣人 - Means 'since he was not a historian of Lu and did not personally see the sage,' a logical conclusion.
  • 传中事迹，皆不足据 - Means 'the events in the text are all unreliable,' a strong criticism.
  • 惟所欲言矣 - Means 'can say whatever they want,' indicating a lack of constraints.
  • 沿及宋季 - Refers to the late Song Dynasty, a specific historical period.
  • 赵鹏飞作春秋经筌 - Refers to Zhao Pengfei's work 'Spring and Autumn Canon,' a specific text.
  • 至不知成风为僖公生母 - Means 'even not knowing that Chengfeng was the birth mother of Duke Xi,' indicating a lack of knowledge.
  • 尚可与论名分，定褒贬乎 - Means 'how can one still discuss titles and determine praise or blame?' a rhetorical question.
  • 元程端学推波助澜，尤为悍戾 - Refers to Cheng Duanxue, who furthered the criticism, indicating a particularly aggressive stance.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 珍为鸿宝 - Treasured as a treasure; '鸿宝' means a great treasure or a valuable work.
  • 竖儒之说 - The words of a petty scholar; '竖儒' is a derogatory term for a shallow or unlearned scholar.
  • 持平之论 - A fair and balanced opinion; '持平' means to be fair and balanced.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 五云多处 - Likely refers to a place or a metaphorical location, possibly a temple or a scholarly gathering place.
  • 原心亭 - A specific place, possibly a pavilion or a temple.
  • 端学春秋解 - Refers to the annotations of the *Spring and Autumn Annals* by a scholar named *端学*.
  • 周编修书昌 - A specific person, likely a scholar or official named *周书昌*.
  • 武王元女 - The eldest daughter of King Wu of Zhou, who is said to be the goddess of the Eastern Mountain.
  • 叔姬归皕 - Refers to a specific event in the *Spring and Autumn Annals* involving a princess named *叔姬* and her return to her husband's family.

  • 五云多处 - A specific location.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 戰死 - Literally 'died in battle,' indicating a heroic death in combat.
  • 當在 - Means 'should be' or 'is likely to be,' expressing a probable location.
  • 考 - Means 'to examine' or 'to investigate,' often used in historical or scholarly contexts.
  • 據宋史 - Refers to the *History of Song*, a historical record of the Song dynasty.
  • 奉使 - Means 'to be sent on a mission' or 'to be dispatched as an envoy,' referring to official diplomatic duties.
  • 距 - Means 'to be distant from' or 'to be separated by,' indicating a temporal or spatial gap.
  • 似未可據後驳前也 - A complex phrase meaning 'it seems unwise to rely on later sources to contradict earlier ones,' suggesting a cautious approach to historical evidence.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 楊令公 - The title '令公' is a respectful term for a high-ranking official or general, often used posthumously. Here, it refers to General Yang Ye, known for his loyalty and bravery.
  • 宋史 - The *History of Song* is a comprehensive historical record compiled during the Yuan dynasty, covering the history of the Song dynasty.
  • 托克托 - The name '托克托' is a transliteration of the Mongolian name 'Toghtoa,' who was a prominent official in the Yuan dynasty. The note in the text explains that the name was initially transcribed as '脱脱' but was corrected to '托克托' for accuracy.
  • 元季 - Refers to the late Yuan dynasty, a period of political and social turmoil.
  • 三史國語解 - Refers to the *Three Histories National Language Explanation*, a work that provides explanations of historical terms and names in the context of the Three Histories (the *History of Song*, *History of Liao*, and *History of Jin*).

Proper Nouns

  • 楊令公 - General Yang Ye, a Song dynasty military leader.
  • 古北口 - An ancient pass in northern China.
  • 宋史 - The *History of Song*.
  • 顧亭林 - Gu Tinglin, a scholar and historian.
  • 昌平山水記 - *Chu Ping Shan Shui Ji*, a work by Gu Tinglin.
  • 王曾 - Wang Zeng, a Song dynasty official.

  • 托克托 - Toghtoa, a prominent official in the Yuan dynasty.
  • 三史國語解 - *Three Histories National Language Explanation*.

[660]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 校勘秘籍 - To proofread or collate secret or rare texts. '校勘' is a technical term for textual collation and correction, often used in the context of classical manuscript scholarship.
  • 四至避暑山庄 - To have visited the Summer Palace four times. '四至' means 'visited four times.'
  • 丁未以冬，戊申以秋，己酉以夏，壬子以春 - A chronological enumeration of visits to the Summer Palace, each marked by a stem-branch year and season. The years are in the traditional Chinese sexagenary cycle.
  • 四时之胜胥览焉 - 'All the beauties of the four seasons were seen.' '胥' here is used as a particle to indicate 'all' or 'each,' and '览' means to view or see.
  • 山容水意，皆出天然 - 'The mountain's form and the water's spirit all arise naturally.' A poetic way to describe natural scenery.
  • 树色泉声，都非尘境 - 'The color of the trees and the sound of the springs are not of the mundane world.' '尘境' refers to the worldly or mundane realm.
  • 阴晴朝暮，千态万状 - 'Whether cloudy or sunny, morning or evening, there are a thousand forms and ten thousand appearances.' A vivid description of changing landscapes.
  • 虽一鸟一花，亦皆入画 - 'Even a single bird or flower is like a painting.' A metaphor for natural beauty.
  • 细草沿坡带谷，皆茸茸如绿氍 - 'The fine grass along the slopes and valleys is all soft and smooth like green felt.' '绿氍' is a metaphor for a green felt-like surface.
  • 高不数寸，齐如裁剪 - 'No more than a few inches high, and as uniform as if cut.' Describes the uniformity of the grass.
  • 无一茎参差长短者 - 'Not a single stem is uneven in length.' Emphasizes the precision of the grass's growth.
  • 苑丁谓之规矩草 - 'The garden attendants call it 'regulation grass.' '苑丁' refers to garden workers or attendants, and '规矩草' is a name given to the grass due to its uniformity.
  • 出宫墙才数步，即眀盽滋蔓矣 - 'Only a few steps beyond the palace walls, and it immediately begins to grow and spread.' '眀盽' is likely a misspelling or variant of '明旺' (clear and vigorous), or '明盛' (proliferating).'
  • 岂非天生嘉卉，以等宸游哉 - 'Is it not that this fine plant was born by heaven to accompany the imperial tour?' '嘉卉' means fine or beautiful plants; '宸游' refers to the emperor's journey or tour.
  • 李又聃先生言 - 'Mr. Li Youdan said.' A standard introductory phrase used to cite a person’s statement.
  • 授徒村落，岑寂寡睧 - 'Taught students in the village, living in quiet seclusion with few visitors.' '寡睧' is a rare term; likely meaning 'few visitors' or 'lacking company.'
  • 偶散步场圃间 - 'Once, while walking in the fields.' '场圃' refers to a field or farm.
  • 遇一士，甚温雅 - 'Met a gentleman, very refined and elegant.' '士' refers to a scholar or gentleman.
  • 各道姓名，颇相款洽 - 'They exchanged names, and became quite friendly.' '款洽' means to be cordial or friendly.
  • 得君如空谷之足音也 - 'Meeting you is like hearing footsteps in the empty valley.' A metaphor for a rare and welcome encounter.
  • 因共至塾 - 'So they went together to the school.' '塾' refers to a private school or study hall.
  • 童子方读孝经 - 'The students were reading the *Classic of Filial Piety*.' '童子' refers to young students or pupils.
  • 此书有今文古文，以何为是 - 'This book has both current and ancient versions. Which one is correct?' '今文古文' refers to the different textual versions of the *Classic of Filial Piety*.
  • 司马贞言之详矣 - 'Sima Zhen has spoken of this in detail.' Sima Zhen was a Tang dynasty scholar known for commentary on the *Book of Han*.
  • 近读吕氏春秋，见审微篇中引诸侯一章，乃是今文 - 'Recently, while reading the *Lüshi Chunqiu*, I saw that the *Chapter on the Lords* in the *Examine the Minute* section is in the current version.' '吕氏春秋' is the *Lüshi Chunqiu*, a classic text.
  • 七国时人所见如是，何处更有古文乎 - 'As seen by people of the Warring States period, where else could there be an ancient version?' This is a rhetorical question.
  • 其人喜曰：君真读书人也 - 'That person said with joy: 'You are truly a scholar!'' '读书人' is a term for a learned person or scholar.
  • 自是屡至塾，张欲报谒，辄谢以贫无栖止 - 'From then on, he often visited the school. Zhang wanted to visit him in return, but he always declined, citing poverty and lack of accommodation.'
  • 夫妇赁住一破屋，无地延客 - 'They rented a dilapidated house, with no room to entertain guests.' '赁住' means to rent and live in; '延客' is to entertain or host guests.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 避暑山庄 - The Summer Palace (or more accurately, the 'Rehe Summer Palace' or 'Bishu Shanzhuang'), a famous imperial retreat located in Rehe (modern Chengde, Hebei province), built during the Qing dynasty.
  • 文津阁 - A famous library and study hall within the Summer Palace, known as one of the four major libraries of the Qing dynasty.
  • 苑丁 - Garden attendants or caretakers of the imperial gardens.
  • 规矩草 - A name given to a particular type of grass due to its uniformity and regularity of growth.
  • 宸游 - A term referring to the emperor's travels or imperial tour.
  • 李又聃先生 - 'Mr. Li Youdan' – a name of a scholar or literary figure; no widely known historical figure by this name, so it should be treated as a proper name.
  • 张子克 - 'Zhang Zike' – another proper name, likely a scholar or teacher.
  • 孝经 - The *Classic of Filial Piety*, one of the Confucian classics.
  • 司马贞 - Sima Zhen (689–760 CE), a Tang dynasty scholar and commentator on the *Book of Han*.
  • 吕氏春秋 - The *Lüshi Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals of Lord Lu), a classical text compiled by the philosopher Lü Buwei during the Warring States period.
  • 审微篇 - A chapter in the *Lüshi Chunqiu*, possibly titled 'Examine the Minute' or 'Investigate the Minute,' referring to a section that analyzes small details.
  • 诸侯一章 - A passage about the feudal lords or rulers.
  • 今文古文 - 'Current text' vs. 'Ancient text' – a textual distinction in classical Chinese literature, often referring to different versions or recensions of a text.
  • 七国时 - The Warring States period, a time of political fragmentation in ancient China (475–221 BCE).
  • 塾 - A private school or study hall, often run by a scholar or teacher.
  • 童子 - Young students, often under the age of 15, who studied the Confucian classics.
  • 读书人 - A term for a scholar or person engaged in literary study, often used to denote a literate or educated person.

[661]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一夕 - Means 'one night' or 'one evening,' setting the temporal context.
  • 忽问 - Means 'suddenly asked,' indicating a sudden and unexpected question.
  • 人未离形之鬼 - Spirits that have not yet departed from their physical form or ghosts that are still attached to their bodies.
  • 鬼已离形之人耳 - People who have already departed from their physical form or those who are already ghosts.
  • 虽未见之，然觉无可畏 - Although I have not seen them, I feel there is nothing to fear.
  • 其人恧然 - The person felt ashamed or the person blushed.
  • 身即是鬼 - I am a ghost.
  • 以生为士族 - Because I was born into a noble family.
  • 不能逐焰口，争钱米 - Unable to follow the flames (i.e., unable to perform the ritual of feeding the hungry ghosts) and compete for money and grain.
  • 叨为气类 - Gratefully being part of the same kind or gratefully being a part of the same species.
  • 求君一饭可乎 - May I ask for a meal from you?
  • 张契分既深 - Zhang's relationship with the person is deep.
  • 亦无疑惧 - Also without doubt or fear.
  • 即为具食 - Immediately prepared food.
  • 且邀使数来 - And invited him to come several times.
  • 考论图籍 - Discuss and study books and documents.
  • 殊有端委 - Very organized or very well-structured.
  • 偶论太极无极之旨 - Occasionally discussing the principles of Taiji and Wuji.
  • 其人怫然 - The person became angry or the person was indignant.
  • 于传有之 - There is a saying or there is a tradition.
  • 天道远，人事迩 - The ways of heaven are distant, while human affairs are near.
  • 六经所论，皆人事 - The Six Classics discuss all human affairs.
  • 即易阐阴阳，亦以天道明人事也 - Even though the *Yi Jing* explains yin and yang, it also uses the ways of heaven to clarify human affairs.
  • 舍人事而言天道，已为虚杳 - To talk about the ways of heaven instead of human affairs is already too abstract and vague.
  • 又推及先天之先，空言聚讼，安用此为 - Furthermore, discussing what comes before the primordial, empty words lead to disputes; what use is there in this?
  • 谓君留心古义 - You pay attention to ancient meanings.
  • 故就君求食 - That's why I come to you for food.
  • 君所见乃如此乎 - Is this how you see things?
  • 拂衣竟起 - He brushed off his clothes and stood up.
  • 倏已影灭 - Immediately disappeared.
  • 再于相遇处候之 - Waited again at the place where they met.
  • 不复睹矣 - But did not see him again.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 张 - Likely refers to Zhang, a character in the story.
  • 太极无极 - Refers to the philosophical concepts of Taiji (Supreme Ultimate) and Wuji (Infinite), which are central in Chinese metaphysics.
  • 六经 - Refers to the Six Classics: the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), *Shujing* (Book of Documents), *Yijing* (Book of Changes), *Liji* (Book of Rites), *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals), and *Lunyu* (Analects of Confucius).
  • 士族 - Refers to a noble or scholarly family.
  • 逐焰口 - Refers to the ritual of feeding the hungry ghosts, a Buddhist practice.
  • 气类 - Refers to the same kind or species, often used in a metaphorical sense.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '一夕，忽问君畏鬼乎？' is elliptical and requires the implied subject to be made explicit in English.
  • The sentence '张曰：人未离形之鬼，鬼已离形之人耳，虽未见之，然觉无可畏。' is a direct quote and must be rendered as such.
  • The sentence '其人恧然曰：身即是鬼，以生为士族，不能逐焰口，争钱米，叨为气类，求君一饭可乎？' is another direct quote and must be rendered as such.
  • The sentence '张契分既深，亦无疑惧，即为具食，且邀使数来，考论图籍，殊有端委。' describes a series of actions and must be read as a coherent sequence.
  • The long sentence '偶论太极无极之旨，其人怫然曰：于传有之，天道远，人事迩，六经所论，皆人事，即易阐阴阳，亦以天道明人事也，舍人事而言天道，已为虚杳：又推及先天之先，空言聚讼，安用此为？谓君留心古义，故就君求食，君所见乃如此乎？拂衣竟起，倏已影灭，再于相遇处候之，不复睹矣。' contains multiple clauses and must be read as a coherent narrative.

Proper Nouns

  • 张 - The name of the character in the story.
  • 太极无极 - Philosophical concepts.
  • 六经 - The Six Classics.
  • 士族 - A noble or scholarly family.
  • 逐焰口 - A ritual practice.
  • 气类 - A metaphorical term.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 督學 - Means 'supervising education' or 'educational inspector,' a specific official role in the imperial examination system.
  • 院吏 - Refers to an official or clerk in the educational bureau.
  • 雍正中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Yongzheng of the Qing Dynasty.
  • 學使 - An official title, referring to the provincial examination examiner.
  • 姬 - A term for a concubine or mistress.
  • 偶失足 - Literally 'accidentally stumbled,' used here to suggest a possible accidental fall.
  • 賣諸 - Means 'sold to' or 'sold among.'
  • 泣徹夜 - Means 'cried all night,' emphasizing the intensity of their emotional state.
  • 啮臂为志 - A traditional expression meaning 'to bite their arms to mark their commitment,' indicating a deep bond between the couple.
  • 不敢近相视 - Means 'dared not approach to look at each other,' showing the fear and respect they had for each other.
  • 挥涕而已 - Means 'only shedding tears,' indicating a restrained expression of emotion.
  • 内外隔绝 - Means 'separated by inner and outer boundaries,' referring to the social and spatial separation between the concubine and her husband.
  • 无由通问 - Means 'no way to communicate,' highlighting the inability to contact each other.
  • 病死 - Means 'died of illness,' indicating the cause of death.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 雍正中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Yongzheng (1722-1735), a period known for its strict governance and reforms.
  • 学使 - The provincial examination examiner, a high-ranking official responsible for overseeing the imperial examinations.
  • 幕友 - A term for a private secretary or advisor, often employed by officials to assist with various tasks.
  • 贩鬻妇女者 - Refers to individuals involved in the trade of women, often associated with human trafficking or prostitution.
  • 官媒家 - Refers to the official matchmaker's house, a place where women were often sold or matched for marriage.
  • 内外隔绝 - Reflects the social hierarchy and gender segregation in traditional Chinese society, where interactions between men and women were strictly regulated.

Proper Nouns

  • 余 - The narrator, an educational inspector.
  • 院吏 - An official or clerk in the educational bureau.
  • 雍正中 - The reign of Emperor Yongzheng.
  • 学使 - The provincial examination examiner.
  • 闽中 - Refers to Fujian Province.
  • 山东 - Refers to Shandong Province.
  • 京师 - Refers to the capital city, Beijing.
  • 官媒家 - The official matchmaker's house.

[663]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 妇闻婢媪道其姓名籍贯，形状年齿 - Classical narrative phrase describing how the woman learns about her husband through details provided by maidservants, including his name, hometown, appearance, and age.
  • 始知之 - Means 'she finally knew' or 'she came to understand,' indicating a moment of realization.
  • 时方坐笔捧楼上，凝立良久 - Describes the woman sitting at her desk in the upstairs room, standing silently for a long time, conveying a sense of deep thought or emotional struggle.
  • 忽对众备言始末，长号数声，奋身投下死 - Dramatic sequence where the woman suddenly recounts her entire story to the crowd, cries out loudly, and then leaps to her death, capturing the intensity of her emotional and physical reaction.
  • 睩柱纲常 - Phrase referring to upholding moral and social norms, often associated with Confucian values.
  • 宁死不辱 - Means 'rather die than be disgraced,' reflecting a strong moral stance.
  • 忍耻偷生 - Means 'to endure shame and live on,' suggesting a compromise of dignity for survival.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • Rou Ting, a scholar in the Hanlin Academy, once visited a friend in Handan. When he arrived, his host was not home, so he temporarily stayed at the City God Temple. Just then, a seller of melons rested his load and lay down in front of the deity's seat. An old man selling thread was staying inside the temple. The old man warned the seller of melons, 'You should not do that; the god is divine.' The seller of melons replied, 'How can the god be in this dilapidated house?' The old man said, 'I often go out at night to cool off and hear voices in the hall. Tiptoeing and listening stealthily, I heard a fox pleading with the god. The general meaning is that a fox from a neighboring house seduced a young man. Just before the young man died, the fox still wanted to take his essence. His family was very angry and set up hunters with guns and arrows to attack it. The fox was frightened, revealed its true form, and ran away. The crowd shouted and followed behind. The fox did not go to its own den but went to a den about a mile away. The people set up nets outside the den, smoked it with fire, and all in the den were killed. But this fox took advantage of the opportunity and escaped. Therefore, it accused them of shifting blame.'

  • 铳矢 - Firearms and arrows.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 世传推命始于李虚中 - It is said that the practice of fortune-telling began with Li Xuzhong.
  • 其法用年月日而不用时 - His method uses the year, month, and day but not the hour.
  • 盖据昌黎所作虚中墓志也 - This is based on the epitaph written by Changli (Han Yu) for Li Xuzhong.
  • 其书宋史艺文志著录 - His book is recorded in the *Yiwenzhi* (Catalogue of Arts and Literature) of the *Songshi* (History of Song).
  • 今已久佚 - It has been lost for a long time.
  • 惟永乐大典载虚中命书三卷 - Only the *Yongle Dadian* (Great Canon of the Yongle Era) contains three volumes of Li Xuzhong's *Ming Shu* (Book of Destiny).
  • 尚为完帙 - It is still a complete work.
  • 所说实兼论八字 - What it says actually includes discussions of the eight characters (Ba Zi).
  • 非不用时 - It is not that the hour is not used.
  • 或疑为宋人所伪托 - Some suspect it was fabricated by Song dynasty people.
  • 莫能明也 - It cannot be proven otherwise.
  • 考虚中墓志 - Examining the epitaph of Li Xuzhong.
  • 称其最深于五行 - It states that he was most proficient in the Five Elements.
  • 书以人始生之年月日，所直日辰，支干相生，胜衰死生，互相斟酌 - He recorded the year, month, and day of a person's birth, the day and hour they were born, the relationships between the stems and branches, and the cycles of growth and decline, birth and death, all of which he carefully considered.
  • 推人寿夭贵贱，利不利云云 - He deduced a person's lifespan, wealth, status, and fortune or misfortune, etc.
  • 天有十二辰 - There are twelve hours in a day.
  • 故一日分为十二时 - Therefore, a day is divided into twelve hours.
  • 日至某辰即某时也 - When the sun reaches a certain hour, it is that hour.
  • 故时亦谓之日辰 - Therefore, the hour is also called a day and hour.
  • 国语：星与日辰之位，皆在北维是也 - Refers to a passage in the *Guoyu* (Discourses of the States) that discusses the positions of stars and day-hours in the northern sky.
  • 诗：跂彼织女，终日七襄 - Refers to a verse in the *Shijing* (Book of Songs) that describes the weaving maiden and her daily tasks.
  • 孔颖达疏，从旦暮七辰一移，因谓之七襄 - Kong Yingda's commentary explains that the weaving maiden moves through seven hours from morning to evening, hence the term 'seven shifts'.
  • 楚辞：吉日兮辰良，王逸注，日谓甲乙辰，谓寅卯以辰，与日分言，尤为明白 - Refers to a passage in the *Chuci* (Songs of Chu) with a note by Wang Yi that clarifies the distinction between days and hours.
  • 据此以推 - Based on this, we can infer.
  • 似乎所直日辰四字，当连上年月日为句 - It seems that the four characters '所直日辰' should be connected to the preceding '年月日' as a single phrase.
  • 后人误属下文为句，故有不用时之说耳 - Later people incorrectly connected it to the following text, thus leading to the idea that the hour was not used.

  • 宋史艺文志 - The *Yiwenzhi* (Catalogue of Arts and Literature) in the *Songshi* (History of Song), a historical record of literary works.
  • 永乐大典 - The *Yongle Dadian* (Great Canon of the Yongle Era), a massive compendium compiled during the Ming dynasty.
  • 八字 - Ba Zi, a system of Chinese astrology based on the eight characters of a person's birth date and time.
  • 国语 - The *Guoyu* (Discourses of the States), an ancient Chinese historical text.
  • 诗 - The *Shijing* (Book of Songs), an ancient Chinese collection of poetry.
  • 孔颖达 - Kong Yingda, a Tang dynasty scholar and commentator on the *Shijing*.
  • 楚辞 - The *Chuci* (Songs of Chu), a collection of poems from the Warring States period.
  • 王逸 - Wang Yi, a Han dynasty scholar and commentator on the *Chuci*.

  • 禀天命，属星辰 - Refers to the idea that one's fate is determined by the stars.

  • 受命既定，即鬼神不能改易，而圣智不能回 - Means 'once fate is determined, neither spirits nor wise men can change it'.
  • 天施气而众星布精 - Means 'heaven imparts its qi (vital energy), and the stars distribute their essence'.
  • 含气而长，得贵则贵，得贱则贱 - Means 'those who contain qi grow, and those who receive nobility become noble, and those who receive lowliness become lowly'.

  • 皆星位大小尊卑之所授 - Means 'all these are bestowed by the positions of the stars'.
  • 是以星言命，古已有之 - Means 'therefore, speaking of fate through the stars has existed since ancient times'.
  • 不必定始于张果 - Means 'it does not necessarily start with Zhang Guo'.
  • 又韩昌黎三星行曰 - Refers to a passage from Han Yu (韩愈), a famous Tang Dynasty scholar.
  • 我生之辰，月宿南斗 - Means 'the day I was born, the moon was in the constellation of the Southern Dipper'.
  • 牛奋其角，箕张其口 - Means 'the Ox raises its horns, and the Dipper opens its mouth'.
  • 杜樊川自作墓志曰 - Refers to a self-written epitaph by Du Mu (杜牧), another famous Tang Dynasty poet.
  • 余生于角星昴毕 - Means 'I was born under the constellations of the Horns (角) and the Stars (昴毕)'.
  • 于角为第八宫，曰疾厄宫，亦曰八杀宫 - Means 'in the Horns, it is the eighth house, called the House of Illness and Calamity, also known as the Eighth House of Destruction'.
  • 土星在焉，火星继木星土 - Means 'Jupiter is there, followed by Mars and Saturn'.
  • 杨睻曰 - Refers to a statement by Yang Xuan (杨玄), a scholar.

  • 木为福德大，君子无虞也 - Means 'Jupiter is a great benefactor, and the gentleman has no worries'.
  • 余曰 - Means 'I say'.
  • 湖守不周岁迁舍人 - Means 'the governor of Hu (湖) did not serve for a full year before being promoted to the position of Shiren (舍人)'.
  • 木还福于角足矣 - Means 'Jupiter's blessings in the Horns are sufficient'.

  • 原起于唐 - Means 'originally arose in the Tang Dynasty'.
  • 其法亦与今不异 - Means 'the method is also not different from today'.

  • 决非唐代文字耳 - Means 'they are definitely not texts from the Tang Dynasty'.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 古者大夫祭五祀 - Refers to ancient rituals where high-ranking officials (dàfu, 大夫) would worship five deities (wǔsì, 五祀). This phrase sets up the context for discussing household worship practices.
  • 今人家惟祭灶神 - Indicates the modern practice of only worshipping the Kitchen God (zào shén, 灶神).
  • 若门神，若井神，若厕神，若中癲神 - Lists additional household deities worshipped, including Door Gods, Well Gods, Toilet Gods, and Central Madness Gods. The use of '若' (ruò) here is a connective meaning 'such as' or 'like.'
  • 或祭或不祭矣 - A flexible expression indicating that some families may or may not worship these deities.
  • 但不识天下一灶神欤，一城一乡一灶神欤，抑一家一灶神欤 - A series of rhetorical questions asking whether there is one Kitchen God for the entire world, one per city or village, or one per family. The use of '抑' (yì) here introduces an alternative.
  • 如天下一灶神，如火神之类 - Hypothetical scenarios where the Kitchen God is universal or local, like the Fire God.
  • 必在祀典，今无此祀典也 - A statement that if the Kitchen God were universal, it would be included in official ritual texts (sìdiǎn, 祀典), which it is not.
  • 如一城一乡一灶神，如城隍社公之类 - Another hypothetical scenario where the Kitchen God is local, like the City God or Village God.
  • 必有专祀，今未见处处有专祀也 - Suggests that if the Kitchen God were local, there would be specific rituals for each place, which is not the case.
  • 然则一家一灶神耳 - Concludes that the Kitchen God is likely per family.
  • 又不识天下人家如恒河沙数，天下灶神亦当如恒河沙数 - Raises the question of how many Kitchen Gods there are given the vast number of households, using the metaphor of '恒河沙数' (hénghéshāshù) to emphasize the multitude.
  • 此恒河沙数之灶神，何人为之，何人命之 - Questions who creates and assigns these Kitchen Gods.
  • 神不太多耶 - Asks whether the number of gods is too many.
  • 人家迁徙不常，兴废亦不常 - Notes the frequent movement and changes in household status.
  • 灶神之闲旷者何所归，灶神之新增者何自来 - Questions what happens to idle or newly created Kitchen Gods.
  • 日日铨除移改，神不又太烦耶 - Suggests that the constant creation and reassignment of gods is burdensome.
  • 此诚不可以理解 - Acknowledges that these questions are beyond understanding.
  • 然而遇灶神者，乃时有之 - Contrasts the theoretical questions with the practical experience of encountering the Kitchen God.
  • 余小时见外祖雪峰张公家 - Personal anecdote about the author's childhood experience with his maternal grandfather, Zhang Gong of Xuefeng.
  • 一司爨姬好以秽物扫入灶 - Describes a kitchen maid who habitually sweeps dirty objects into the stove.
  • 夜梦乌衣人呵之，且批其颊 - Narrates the maid's dream of a black-clothed figure rebuking and slapping her.
  • 觉而颊肿成痈，数日巨如杯 - Describes the physical consequences of the dream, with the maid's cheek swelling into a large abscess.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '與鄰女相悅，時相窺也' is somewhat elliptical. It can be read as 'He fell in love with his neighbor's daughter and they often looked at each other.'
  • The phrase '微露盜香跡，女父疑焉' is somewhat ambiguous. It can be read as 'He began to show signs of stealing fragrant things, and the girl's father became suspicious.'
  • The phrase '夜伏牆上，左右顧視' is somewhat elliptical. It can be read as 'At night, he hid on the wall and looked around left and right.'
  • The phrase '陰伺其來往，乃見女室中有一少年' is somewhat elliptical. It can be read as 'He secretly watched their comings and goings, and then he saw a young man in the girl's room.'
  • The phrase '衣飾形貌皆無異，始知男女皆為狐媚也' is somewhat elliptical. It can be read as 'Their clothing and appearance were all the same, and he then realized that both the man and the woman were bewitched by fox spirits.'
  • The phrase '此真黎邱之伎矣，青雷曰' is somewhat elliptical. It can be read as 'This is truly the trick of the fox, Zhu Qinglei said.'
  • The phrase '以我所見，好事者當為媒合' is somewhat elliptical. It can be read as 'According to what I saw, those who like to meddle should arrange a match.'
  • The phrase '然聞兩家父母皆恚甚，各延巫驅狐' is somewhat elliptical. It can be read as 'However, I heard that both sets of parents were very angry, and they each invited a witch to drive away the fox spirits.'
  • The phrase '時方束裝北上，不知究竟如何也' is somewhat elliptical. It can be read as 'At the time, I was packing to go north, and I don't know how it turned out in the end.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 李云举言 - Narrative formula meaning 'Li Yun said' or 'Li Yun recounted'; introduces the speaker or narrator of the story.
  • 薰狐者 - Compound noun meaning 'the fox-scented one' or 'the fox that uses scent'; likely refers to a fox hunter or someone who uses scent to track foxes.
  • 載燧挟罟 - Compound verb meaning 'to carry fire and nets'; describes the equipment of a fox hunter.
  • 墟墓間 - Location phrase meaning 'between villages and tombs'; refers to a liminal, possibly haunted or eerie place.
  • 方巾阑衫人 - Compound noun describing a person in a square headpiece (方巾) and a loose robe (阑衫); likely a scholar or literatus.
  • 酃酃（苦侯反，说文曰：鬼声也，音需） - Rare and unusual onomatopoeic word; defined in the *Shuowen Jiezi* as a ghostly sound, similar to 'xū' (需). Likely a phonetic rendering of a ghostly howl or cry.
  • 群狐四集 - Phrase meaning 'the foxes gathered from all directions'; conveys the sudden and collective appearance of the foxes.
  • 围绕丛薄 - Phrase meaning 'surrounded the dense undergrowth'; '丛薄' refers to thick, tangled vegetation.
  • 狰狞嗥叫 - Vivid compound describing the foxes as 'fearsome' and 'howling' or 'howling in a menacing way.'
  • 齐呼捕此恶人 - Phrase meaning 'all cried out together to capture this evil person'; describes a collective action by the foxes.
  • 煮以作脯 - Phrase meaning 'cook him to make into dried meat'; '脯' is dried meat, often used as food or as a sacrificial offering.
  • 窘急 - Compound meaning 'in a desperate or窘迫 situation, full of urgency.'
  • 俯而号曰 - Phrase meaning 'bowed down and cried out, saying...'; conveys a posture of supplication or desperation.
  • 如蒙见释 - Polite request meaning 'if you would be so kind as to release me.'
  • 不敢再履此地 - Promise meaning 'I will not set foot here again.'
  • 鬼狐具不见 - Phrase meaning 'the ghost and fox both vanished'; '具' indicates both entities disappeared completely.
  • 善了事 - Phrase meaning 'to handle matters well' or 'to deal with things skillfully'; implies the ghost and fox acted in a complete and efficient manner.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 薰狐者 - Refers to a fox hunter, possibly using scent to track foxes; in Chinese folklore, foxes are often associated with supernatural or magical qualities.
  • 方巾阑衫人 - Describes a scholar or literatus; in the context of the story, this person may be a ghost or a supernatural being, possibly a former scholar who has become a spirit.
  • 鬼狐 - Combination of 'ghost' and 'fox'; refers to a supernatural being that is both a ghost and a fox; in Chinese mythology, foxes are often associated with transformation, trickery, and sometimes with spirits or deities.
  • 煮以作脯 - The idea of cooking a human to make dried meat is a common motif in Chinese folklore and religious texts, often symbolizing punishment or transformation.
  • 善了事 - Used to describe the ghost and fox as having handled the situation 'well' or 'efficiently'; suggests the supernatural entities acted decisively and without unnecessary conflict.

Proper Nouns

  • 李云 - Proper name, likely a narrator or storyteller; should be translated as 'Li Yun.'
  • 东光 - Place name, likely a county or region in ancient China; should be translated as 'Dongguang.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 原不難於殺一人 - Means 'originally it was not difficult to kill one person,' suggesting an initial ease in achieving a goal.
  • 然殺一人易，殺一人而激眾人之怒 - Concessive clause meaning 'but killing one person is easy, but killing one person and thereby provoking the anger of many is...' indicating a shift in complexity.
  • 不焚巢犁穴不止也 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'they will not stop until they destroy the nests and plow the holes,' implying total destruction.

  • 召陵之役 - Refers to the Battle of Zhaoling, a historical event where the state of Qi intervened in a conflict between the states of Cai and Chu.
  • 不责以僭王，而责以苞茅 - Means 'not holding them accountable for usurping the title of king, but holding them accountable for failing to provide tribute,' indicating a strategic choice in diplomatic demands.
  • 讲学家说春秋者 - Refers to scholars who interpret the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, a classic text of Chinese historiography.
  • 动议齐桓之小就 - Means 'debating whether Duke Huan of Qi achieved a small victory,' suggesting a critique of his military strategy.
  • 方城汉水之固 - Refers to the natural fortifications of Fangcheng and the Han River, indicating a strategic defensive position.
  • 不识可一战胜乎 - Means 'who knows if one battle could be won,' suggesting doubt about the feasibility of a single victory.
  • 一战而不胜，天下事尚可为乎 - Means 'if one battle is not won, can anything else be done in the world,' indicating a broader concern about the consequences of failure.
  • 淮西符离之事 - Refers to the Battle of Fuli in Huai West, a historical event where the state of Han suffered a significant defeat.
  • 吾征诸史册矣 - Means 'I have verified this in historical records,' indicating a reliance on historical evidence.

  • 讲学家说春秋者 - Scholars who interpret the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, a classic text of Chinese historiography.
  • 动议齐桓之小就 - A critique of Duke Huan of Qi's military strategy, suggesting that his victory was not as significant as it seemed.
  • 方城汉水之固 - The natural fortifications of Fangcheng and the Han River, which were strategically important in ancient Chinese warfare.
  • 淮西符离之事 - The Battle of Fuli in Huai West, where the state of Han suffered a significant defeat.
  • 吾征诸史册矣 - A reference to the historical records that document these events.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 周书昌曰 - Standard Classical Chinese format for quoting or attributing a statement, meaning 'Zhou Shuchang said.'

  • 借宿民舍 - Means 'stayed overnight in a peasant's house.'
  • 窗外老树森翳 - Describes the scene outside the window as 'old trees densely shading the area.'

  • 主人言时闻鬼语 - Means 'the host said that sometimes ghostly voices could be heard.'
  • 不辨所说何事也 - Means 'it was impossible to understand what they were saying.'
  • 是夜月黑 - Means 'that night was moonless.'
  • 果隐隐闻之 - Means 'indeed, I faintly heard it.'
  • 不甚了了 - Means 'not very clearly.'
  • 恐惊之散去 - Means 'fearing that they would be scared away.'
  • 乃启窗潜出 - Means 'I then quietly opened the window and crept out.'

  • 中声 - Refers to the 'middle tone' or moderate style in literature.

  • 排斥不数 - Means 'rejecting or not counting them.'
  • 务言秦汉 - Means 'insisting on speaking of the Qin and Han periods.'
  • 门户之争 - Means 'disputes over schools or factions.'
  • 质文递变 - Means 'the gradual change from substance to style.'
  • 原不一途 - Means 'originally not one path.'
  • 宋末文格猥琐 - Means 'the literary style of the late Song period was vulgar.'
  • 元末文格纤秾 - Means 'the literary style of the late Yuan period was overly ornate.'
  • 力追韩欧 - Means 'striving to emulate Han Yu and Ouyang Xiu.'
  • 舂容大雅 - Means 'majestic and dignified.'
  • 三杨以后 - Refers to 'after the Three Yangs,' a group of officials.

  • 日就肤廓 - Means 'gradually becoming superficial and empty.'
